critic_raw:
The split strategy describes grouping patients 'by their treatment initiation date to approximate distribution akin to a temporal setting' while simultaneously applying stratified random sampling. These are contradictory: temporal grouping would require a sequential cutoff, but stratified random sampling pulls patients from all time points into each split. The design cannot be both temporal and random simultaneously. The likely implementation is random, which means patients from the same clinical time period appear in both training and test, and any temporal shift in treatment protocols is not captured.

The multi-task learning setup is evaluated using average precision (AP) as the primary metric, but model selection on the validation set uses F1. This mismatch means the model chosen is not optimal for the reported metric. A model that maximizes F1 (which depends on a classification threshold) may not maximize AP (which summarizes the full precision-recall curve). The two can diverge substantially in imbalanced settings, particularly when the label prevalence differs across the three treatment types.

Patients are labeled only for treatments they received, introducing treatment-selection bias. Patients who received immunotherapy are systematically different from those who received chemotherapy (oncologists assign treatment based on tumor biology and patient factors that are themselves partially captured in the genomic features). The multi-task model trained to predict response across all three heads will learn treatment-selection patterns conflated with treatment-response patterns, and the advantage over the EHR-only baseline may reflect better prediction of treatment assignment rather than response.

The 20 interaction features (gene expression × mutation status) are constructed and described as 'derived,' but the design does not specify whether these are prespecified or selected from data. If they are selected from training data based on correlation with labels (which the '550 total features' count suggests is tightly curated), this is post-hoc feature engineering that inflates the multi-task model's advantage.
all_issues_raised: The split strategy is internally contradictory: it simultaneously claims temporal grouping and stratified random sampling, which are mutually exclusive; the likely random implementation removes the temporal control the design claims to provide.

Model selection uses validation F1 but the primary reported metric is average precision (AP); these can diverge substantially under class imbalance, so the selected model is not optimal for the claimed primary metric.

Treatment-selection bias is unaddressed: patients are labeled only for received treatments, and treatment assignment is correlated with genomic features; the multi-task model may be learning treatment selection rather than treatment response, inflating its apparent advantage over the EHR-only baseline.

The 20 interaction features (gene expression × mutation status) may be data-selected rather than prespecified; if chosen based on training-set correlation with outcomes, they inflate the multi-task model's feature advantage over the baseline.

Model selection uses validation F1 but the primary reported metric is average precision (AP); these can diverge substantially under class imbalance, so the selected model is not optimal for the claimed primary metric.

Treatment-selection bias is unaddressed: patients are labeled only for received treatments, and treatment assignment is correlated with genomic features; the multi-task model may be learning treatment selection rather than treatment response, inflating its apparent advantage over the EHR-only baseline.

The 20 interaction features (gene expression × mutation status) may be data-selected rather than prespecified; if chosen based on training-set correlation with outcomes, they inflate the multi-task model's feature advantage over the baseline.
